docs.metaDescription: The open AT Protocol record schemas behind GainForest — biodiversity observations, impact claims, funding, and identity.
docs.title: Data schemas
docs.lead: The open record formats behind GainForest. Every observation, project, certificate, donation, and post is stored as a structured record using the schemas below.

sections.feed: Social primitives ported from Bluesky into the GainForest commons — posts (and reply comments) and likes, so the feed can be posted to, commented on, and liked.
sections.dwc: Biodiversity occurrence records aligned with the TDWG Simple Darwin Core standard. Occurrences reference a shared event; measurements reference an occurrence.
sections.ac: Media and passive-acoustic-monitoring records aligned with TDWG Audiovisual Core. Audio and multimedia link to a Darwin Core occurrence as evidence.
sections.organization: Organization map-data records — published map layers (drone orthomosaics, GeoJSON overlays) and the named monitored areas that group repeat captures into change-over-time series.
sections.certified: Identity primitives from the Hypercerts ecosystem — actor profiles, organizations, follows, badges, and locations.
sections.hypercerts: Impact claims (activity, contribution, contributor, rights), collections, funding receipts, attachments, work scope, and shared types.

docs.epdsLinkDesc: An interactive tour of the login system and the servers behind these records.
docs.cgsLinkDesc: An interactive tour of the guard service that lets a whole team share one account.
docs.atprotoLinkDesc: An interactive tour of the open network everything here is built on.
docs.teeEpdsLinkDesc: See how private keys can stay inside protected hardware.

guides.audiomothTitle: AudioMoth spectrogram labels as Darwin Core occurrences
guides.audiomothDescription: How each confirmed time and frequency box becomes a new occurrence linked to its source audio recording.
guides.guideIntro: This guide defines the application-level conventions used with the occurrence schema. The schema remains generic Darwin Core; the guide explains how AudioMoth recordings, spectrogram boxes, and occurrence records connect.

meta.title: Tainá field assistant
meta.description: Connect your own Telegram bot and turn what you see in nature into verifiable biodiversity observations.

hero.title: Meet Tainá
hero.description: Tainá is your Telegram field companion. Send her a photo or a note about a plant, animal, or fungus — she identifies the species and records the observation under your GainForest account.
hero.step1Description: Create a private Telegram bot with @BotFather and link it here. Only you can talk to it.
hero.step2Description: Message your bot whatever you see in the field. Tainá identifies the species and asks for the details that matter.
hero.step3Description: Each confirmed sighting becomes a biodiversity observation saved under your account and visible on your profile.
hero.storageNote: Observations confirmed through Tainá are saved under your GainForest account. Photos and notes are stored with your account files.

signIn.title: Sign in to get started
signIn.description: Tainá records observations under your GainForest account, so sign in before connecting your bot.

setup.title: Connect your Tainá bot
setup.description: Create a Telegram bot for Tainá and paste its token below. The bot is private — it works only for you.
setup.howTitle: How to create your bot (about 2 minutes)
setup.howStep3: Copy the token it gives you (it looks like 123456789:ABC…).
setup.howStep4: Paste it below — this bot is used only by you.

errors.invalidToken: That doesn't look like a bot token. Copy it from @BotFather (it looks like 123456789:ABC…).
errors.genericError: We couldn't connect your bot. Please try again.
errors.runtimeUnreachable: Tainá is unreachable right now. Please try again in a moment.

activation.title: Activate your bot
activation.description: Your bot is connected but still private. Open it in Telegram and send this one-time code so only you can talk to it.
activation.hint: The button sends the code for you — or message the bot and type the code yourself.

linked.title: Your Tainá bot
linked.justLinkedNote: Your bot is linked and ready.
linked.description: You're running <bot></bot>. Open it in Telegram to log what you see, or review everything on your dashboard.

dashboard.loadError: We couldn't load your Tainá dashboard. Please try again.
dashboard.notLinkedDescription: You haven't connected a Telegram bot yet. Set one up and Tainá will start logging your observations here.
dashboard.activationDescription: <bot></bot> is connected but private. Open it in Telegram and send this one-time code so only you can talk to it.
dashboard.apiKeyDescription: A GainForest AI agent key created for Tainá. It also appears in Settings → AI agent keys, marked as Tainá's. Keep it secret.
dashboard.revokeConfirm: Revoke this key? Tainá will stop recording observations until you generate a new one.
dashboard.keyActionFailed: That didn't work — please try again.
dashboard.chatEmpty: No messages yet. Open your bot in Telegram and describe or photograph something you saw — your conversation with Tainá shows up here live.
dashboard.restartConfirm: Start a fresh conversation with Tainá? She'll forget this chat's history — your recorded observations are not affected.
dashboard.restartFailed: The restart didn't work — please try again.
dashboard.resetConfirm: Reset your Tainá agent? This disconnects your bot, deletes its chat history and your profile here, and revokes its key. Your recorded observations are not affected. You can set Tainá up again anytime.
dashboard.resetFailed: The reset didn’t work — please try again.
dashboard.profileDescription: A short Markdown profile stored with your agent so Tainá knows who you are. It shapes how she talks with you and the connections she can suggest.
dashboard.profilePromptIntro: Copy this prompt into ChatGPT or Claude to draft a USER.md profile, then send the Markdown it returns to your agent on Telegram — or paste it below.
dashboard.profilePromptBody:
Create a two-page, non-sensitive connection profile that helps people or agents find good reasons to talk with me, collaborate, trade notes, invite me into something, or introduce me to someone unexpected.

Don’t make it a resume, biography, personality type, or definitive account. Make it warm, specific, lightly imaginative, and useful for serendipity.

Draw out my recurring curiosities, live questions, tastes, projects, values-in-practice, conversation surfaces, possible offers/asks, and the kinds of people, scenes, collaborations, or opportunities I might resonate with. Include work, but don’t make work the whole frame.

Stay grounded: distinguish evidence from hunches, and phrase uncertain things as possibilities or questions. Don’t infer sensitive or private facts. Include what not to assume, and where more context would help.
dashboard.profilePlaceholder: Paste the Markdown profile here. Leave out secrets and anything you do not want stored with your agent.
dashboard.profileCounter: This becomes USER.md in your agent’s workspace. {count}/{max} characters.
dashboard.profileSaved: Saved — Tainá will use it from her next reply.
dashboard.profileSaveFailed: We couldn’t save your profile. Please try again.

meta.title: Submit field data
meta.description: Send large batches of field photos and survey exports to the GainForest team for review and publishing.

hero.title: Submit your field data in one big batch
hero.description: Pack your photos and survey exports (for example from KoboToolbox) into a single zip file and send it to us. Our team reviews every batch and publishes the observations to your account for you.
hero.step1Description: Photos, spreadsheets and survey exports — the format doesn't need to be perfect.
hero.step2Description: Files up to 10GB upload straight from your browser. Keep the page open until it finishes.
hero.step3Title: We review and publish for you
hero.step3Description: The GainForest team checks the data and publishes the observations to your account. You can follow the progress here.

signIn.title: Sign in to submit data
signIn.description: Batches are linked to your account so the observations end up in the right place.

form.consentHint: You can withdraw this permission at any time in Settings.

meta.title: AudioMoth recorder setup
meta.description: Set the clock, configure recording schedules and update firmware on your AudioMoth acoustic recorder, straight from the browser.

audiomoth.title: AudioMoth
audiomoth.subtitle: Plug an AudioMoth into your computer with a USB cable and switch it to USB/OFF. You can set its clock, change its recording settings and update its firmware right here — no extra apps needed.
audiomoth.unsupportedBrowser: This browser can't talk to USB devices. Please use a recent version of Chrome or Edge on a computer.

connection.noDeviceBody: Connect your AudioMoth via USB, set its switch to USB/OFF, then press the button to grant access.
connection.setupReasonSettings: Recording settings differ from the GainForest setup
connection.setupReasonSettingsUnknown: Recording settings haven't been applied from this computer yet

device.setTimeError: Couldn't set the clock. Check the connection and try again.

configure.scheduleBody: Choose up to four periods each day when the AudioMoth should record.
configure.noPeriodsHint: No recording periods — the device will not record.
configure.disable48DCFilterLabel: Turn off the 48 Hz low-frequency filter
configure.dailyFoldersLabel: Save recordings in a new folder each day
configure.filenameWithDeviceIdLabel: Include device ID in file names
configure.configureSuccess: Settings applied. The device clock was also set.
configure.configureError: The AudioMoth didn't confirm the new settings. Please try again.
configure.extendPrepTimeLabel: Extend WAV file preparation time for slow SD cards
configure.displayVoltageRangeLabel: Use NiMH/LiPo voltage range for battery level indication
configure.magneticSwitchLabel: Enable magnetic switch for delayed start
configure.chimeBody: Options for setting up the device with the AudioMoth mobile app's acoustic chime. Needs firmware 1.12.0 or newer.
configure.requireAcousticConfigLabel: Always require acoustic chime on switching to CUSTOM
configure.requireLocationInChimeLabel: Also require location in acoustic chime
configure.useTimeZoneInChimeLabel: Use time zone in acoustic chime
configure.adjustScheduleFromChimeLabel: Also adjust schedule to new time zone
configure.ignoreExternalMicLabel: Ignore external microphone for acoustic chime
configure.gpsBody: For AudioMoths with the GPS add-on. Needs firmware 1.11.0 or newer.
configure.gpsEnabledLabel: Enable GPS for time and location setting
configure.gpsFixModeLabel: Acquire GPS fix before and after

firmware.releasesError: Couldn't load the release list. You can still use a local file.
firmware.localFileLabel: Or use a firmware file from this computer (.bin)
firmware.invalidFirmwareFile: That file doesn't look like AudioMoth firmware.
firmware.clearSettingsLabel: Also clear the stored recording settings
firmware.noFirmwareSelected: Choose an official release or a local firmware file first.
firmware.manualBootloaderRequired: This AudioMoth can't switch to update mode automatically. Please use the desktop Flash App with the switch in USB/OFF while pressing the SWITCH button.

firmware.serialUnsupported: This browser can't finish the update over serial. Please use Chrome or Edge on a computer.
firmware.flashSuccess: Firmware updated successfully (check code {crc}). The device is restarting — it may take a few seconds to reappear.

autoSetup.subtitle: Sit back — updating firmware, setting the clock, applying field-ready recording settings and saving the device to your equipment list.
autoSetup.firmwareManualSkip: This device needs a manual update — skipped. You can update it from the Firmware tab.
autoSetup.firmwareFailed: The firmware update didn't finish. Detach and reattach your AudioMoth, then try again.
autoSetup.clockSetting: Setting the clock from this computer…
autoSetup.clockFailed: Couldn't set the clock. Check the connection and try again.
autoSetup.settingsDone: 48 kHz, 60 s recordings with 4 min pauses, acoustic chime required
autoSetup.settingsFailed: The AudioMoth didn't confirm the settings. Please try again.

label.subtitle: Review AudioMoth recordings, mark calls by time and frequency, and build a clean label set for your field data.
label.signInBody: Occurrences are saved to your account. Sign in, upload AudioMoth recordings, then mark the wildlife sounds you find.
label.loadFailed: Your recordings could not be loaded. Please try again.
label.occurrencesLoadFailed: Saved occurrences could not be loaded for this recording.
label.noRecordingsBody: The labelling workspace uses recordings already saved to your account so every box can become a linked biodiversity occurrence.
label.savedAtproto: Every saved box is a biodiversity occurrence in your account.
label.deleteConfirm: Delete this occurrence? This removes it from your account and cannot be undone.
label.deleteFailed: The occurrence could not be deleted. Please try again.
label.saveFailed: The occurrence could not be saved. Your box is still here so you can try again.
label.recordedAtMissing: This recording has no valid start time, so an occurrence time cannot be calculated.
label.audioUnavailable: This recording has no playable original or preview. Upload it again with previews enabled.
label.scientificNameHint: Leave blank to save the honest broad group, such as Anura for frogs.
label.saveAtprotoHint: Creates a Darwin Core occurrence linked to this audio recording.
label.stepDraw: Draw a time and frequency box
label.emptyTitle: Label wildlife sounds in your AudioMoth recordings
label.emptyBody: Choose an SD card folder or a set of WAV files. Pick a recording, drag a box around a call, then identify it as a bird, frog, insect, another sound, or a note.
label.privacyNote: Your recordings stay on this device while you label them.
label.workspaceSummary: {files, plural, one {# uploaded recording} other {# uploaded recordings}} · {labels, plural, one {# occurrence here} other {# occurrences here}}
label.savedLocally: Drafts are saved in this browser. Export them when you are ready.
label.selectToEdit: Select a label to edit it
label.labelsEmpty: No labels yet. Drag a box over a sound in the spectrogram to begin.
label.drawHint: Drag across the spectrogram to mark the sound you want to label. The box captures both when it happened and its frequency range.
label.speciesHint: Leave blank if you only know the animal group.
label.notePlaceholder: Call type, confidence, weather, or anything useful…
label.saveHint: Saved in this browser until you export the label set.
label.drawInstruction: Drag to draw · select a box to edit
label.spectrogramFailed: This recording could not be turned into a spectrogram.
label.audioUnsupported: Your browser cannot play this recording.

deployments.intro: Deployments record where and when an AudioMoth was placed in the field. Each one plays an acoustic chime that sets the recorder's clock, location and deployment ID.
deployments.signInTitle: Sign in to see your deployments
deployments.signInBody: Your deployments are saved to your account. Sign in to create and manage them.
deployments.loadError: Something went wrong while loading your deployments. Please try again.
deployments.emptyBody: Create your first deployment to set up an AudioMoth in the field with the acoustic chime.
deployments.deleteFailed: The deployment could not be deleted. Please try again.
deployments.createIntro: Name the site, choose the location, then play the chime with this device's speaker held against the AudioMoth's microphone.
deployments.locationUnavailable: Your location could not be determined. Enter the coordinates by hand.
deployments.equipmentEmpty: No AudioMoths are registered in your equipment or your organizations' yet.
deployments.equipmentHint: Pick the registered AudioMoth you are deploying — including ones registered by your organizations — to link it to this event.
deployments.invalidId: The deployment ID must be 16 characters (0–9, a–f).
deployments.createFailed: The deployment could not be saved. Please try again.
deployments.playFailed: The chime could not be played. Please try again.
deployments.playing: Playing the chime — hold the speaker against the AudioMoth…
deployments.chimeHint: Set the AudioMoth's switch to CUSTOM and hold this device's speaker against it while the chime plays.
deployments.doneBody: The chime was played and the deployment was saved to your account. Play it again if the AudioMoth didn't catch it.
deployments.editIntro: Change the site name or the linked AudioMoth. The deployment ID, location and date are fixed to the chime that was already played.
deployments.updateFailed: The deployment could not be updated. Please try again.

upload.intro: Insert an AudioMoth SD card and choose it below. Recordings are recognised by the deployment ID embedded in each file, kept safe in archival storage and saved to your account.
upload.signInBody: Your recordings are saved to your account. Sign in to upload them.
upload.dropTitle: Choose your SD card or drop it here
upload.dropBody: Pick the SD card drive (or any folder of AudioMoth recordings). Files are read on this device first — nothing is uploaded until you confirm.
upload.noWavBody: No WAV files were found in the selection. Choose the top level of the AudioMoth SD card or a folder that contains recordings.
upload.unmatchedTitle: Deployment not found in your account
upload.noIdTitle: No deployment ID in these recordings
upload.unreadable: {count} files could not be read as WAV recordings and will be skipped.
upload.previewHint: Adds a small preview and a spectrogram of each recording to your account so it can be played and seen anywhere.
upload.doneBody: {count} recordings uploaded. {skipped} already existed and were skipped.
upload.notConfigured: Recording storage is not set up yet. Please contact the team.
upload.uploadFailed: The upload could not be completed. Please try again.
upload.deploymentFallback: Created automatically when uploading SD card recordings.
upload.readFailed: The SD card could not be read. Remove and re-insert it, then try again — or drag the folder onto this page instead.

recordings.title: Recordings
recordings.loadError: Recordings could not be loaded right now.
recordings.empty: No recordings uploaded from this deployment yet.
recordings.previewUnavailable: No preview available for this recording
recordings.accountEmptyBody: Recordings uploaded from an AudioMoth SD card will show up here, grouped by deployment.
recordings.explorerHint: Recorders capture around the clock — pick a day on the calendar, then tap an hour bar to narrow down.

metadata.title: Tainá devices
metadata.description: Field updates from GainForest devices running Tainá, including whether each device is active and ready.

bluesky.onHint: This post will also be shared on Bluesky
bluesky.offHint: Also share this post on Bluesky

consent.title: Share your posts on Bluesky
consent.body: When you post an update here, we can also publish it to Bluesky, an open social network with millions of people.
consent.discovery: It's a great way to get discovered: your updates reach nature lovers, funders, and researchers far beyond GainForest.
consent.profileNote: You don't have a Bluesky profile yet, so we'll create one for you from your GainForest profile (your name, photo, and bio).
consent.profileNoteMaybe: If you don't have a Bluesky profile yet, we'll create one for you from your GainForest profile (your name, photo, and bio).
consent.optOutNote: Only new posts you publish after turning this on are shared. You can turn it off anytime in Settings.
consent.error: We couldn't turn on Bluesky sharing. Please try again.

settings.title: Bluesky
settings.toggleDescription: Community updates you post are also published to your Bluesky account, so more people can discover your work.
settings.error: We couldn't save that change. Please try again.

epds.metaDescription: An interactive tour of ePDS, the extended Personal Data Server that lets anyone join the AT Protocol network with just their email.
epds.title: How ePDS works
epds.lead: GainForest runs on the AT Protocol, the open network behind Bluesky. Joining that network normally takes a password and some technical setup. ePDS removes that wall: you type your email, you get a code, and you are in. This page shows how it works, step by step.

whatIs.heading: What is a PDS, and what does the e add?
whatIs.p1: On the AT Protocol, everyone keeps their data on a Personal Data Server, or PDS. Think of it as a personal home for your posts, observations, and projects. You own what is inside, and you can move to another server at any time without losing anything.
whatIs.p2: ePDS is a normal PDS with one extension: a friendly front door. Instead of asking for a password, it signs you in with a one-time email code or a social account, and it creates an account for you on your first visit. Behind that door everything stays standard AT Protocol, so every app on the network can talk to it.

compare.heading: Two front doors
compare.intro: Same house, different door. Flip the switch and try both ways of signing in.
compare.codeLabel: Type the code from your email
compare.classicCaption: A classic PDS asks for a password, and often for an invite code from the server admin. You also have to create the account first.
compare.epdsCaption: ePDS asks for your email and sends you a code. First visit? Your account is created for you on the spot.

journey.heading: The login journey
journey.intro: Press play, or step through it yourself. The moving dot is your login making its round trip.

s1.title: You click Sign in
s1.text: It all starts in the app you are using, for example GainForest. You click Sign in and maybe type your email.

s2.title: The app calls the PDS
s2.text: Your app quietly asks the PDS to start a login. This is a standard AT Protocol request, the same one any Bluesky app would make.

s3.title: The PDS opens its front door
s3.text: Here is the ePDS trick. Instead of showing a password form, the PDS sends your browser to its friendly login service.

s4.title: A code flies to your inbox
s4.text: The login service creates a one-time code and emails it to you. The code works once and expires after a few minutes.

s5.title: You type the code
s5.text: You copy the code from the email into the login screen. That proves the inbox is yours. No password is ever involved.

s6.title: New here? Pick a handle
s6.text: On your first visit, ePDS creates an account for you and asks you to pick a handle, your public name on the network.

s7.title: The signed handshake
s7.text: The login service tells the PDS that you are verified. The message carries a cryptographic signature, so nobody can fake it.

s8.title: Back to your app
s8.text: The PDS sends your browser back to your app with a short-lived code. Your app trades that code for an access token.

s9.title: You are in
s9.text: Your app can now read and write your data on your behalf. To the rest of the network you look like any other AT Protocol user.

map.heading: The moving parts
map.intro: ePDS is a small team of services. Tap each one to see its job.

app.name: Your app
app.desc: Any AT Protocol app, like GainForest or Bluesky. It never sees your email code or any keys. It only receives the final access token.

pds.name: PDS core
pds.desc: A standard Personal Data Server. It stores your records, talks to the rest of the network, and issues tokens. ePDS wraps it without changing how it speaks to other apps.

auth.name: Login service
auth.desc: The friendly front door. It shows the login screen, sends the email codes, handles social logins, and helps new users pick a handle.

email.name: Email provider
email.desc: Delivers the one-time codes to your inbox. Each code is short-lived and can be used exactly once.

db.name: Shared database
db.desc: A small database both services share. It keeps track of login sessions and codes, nothing more.

more.heading: Keep exploring
more.lexiconsDesc: The open record formats that live inside each PDS.
more.githubDesc: The full source code, open for anyone to read, run, and improve.
more.cgsDesc: The guard service that lets a whole team share one group account safely.
more.atprotoDesc: An interactive tour of the open network everything here is built on.
more.teeDesc: See how private keys can stay inside protected hardware.
more.routerDesc: How apps find the right server for an email when a community runs several.

epdsRouter.metaDescription: An interactive guide to the ePDS router: how an app finds the right account for an email login across many servers. Hosts share only one-way fingerprints, never credentials or addresses.
epdsRouter.title: One email, many servers
epdsRouter.lead: ePDS lets you sign in with just your email. When a community runs several servers, an app first needs to answer a small question: which accounts exist for this email? The router answers it in a single call. Nobody hands over an address or a password.

routing.title: One lookup, the right account
routing.text: An app asks once and learns which accounts exist for the email. Each answer is an identity the network can always locate, even after a server move.

privacy.title: Emails are never stored
privacy.text: Addresses are scrambled on their own server before anything leaves it. The router only ever sees fingerprints, and it scrambles them again before storing.

zerochange.title: Hosts stay in control
zerochange.text: No credentials to hand over, nothing to install inside the PDS. Hosts run one small script next to their server, on their own schedule.

problem.heading: Why discovery is needed
problem.p1: An app with email sign-in has to send the login to one specific server. That server emails the code and holds the account. With a single server the answer is fixed. With several servers the app needs a directory. Email addresses are exactly the kind of data you do not want to copy into one big list.
problem.p2: The router solves this with a small trick: it works only with fingerprints. Each server scrambles its addresses one-way at home and shares nothing else. Each lookup scrambles the incoming email the same way and compares fingerprints. Same answer, no address book, and nobody hands anyone their keys.

demo.heading: Try a lookup
demo.intro: Three example servers, three example emails. Pick one and watch how the app finds the right account.
demo.ariaLabel: Email lookup and identity resolution diagram

idle.title: Pick an email above
idle.text: The app wants to start an email sign-in. It does not yet know which account this email belongs to.

sent.title: The app asks the router
sent.text: It sends the email over a private, authenticated call. Lookups are never written to any log.

fingerprint.title: The router checks fingerprints
fingerprint.text: The email becomes a fingerprint on arrival. The router compares it against each server's set of fingerprints.

answer.title: The router answers with identities
answer.text: The answer is not a server address. It is the account's permanent identity, called a DID. The router's job ends here.

resolve.title: The network finds the home
resolve.text: The app asks the identity directory where this identity lives today. This is standard AT Protocol resolution and it stays correct even after an account moves servers.

one.title: One account found
one.text: The directory points at the account's current home server. The sign-in starts there and the code lands in the right inbox.

many.title: Two accounts found
many.text: This email has two separate identities. The app shows a chooser, and each identity resolves to its own home server.

none.title: No account yet
none.text: No identity exists for this email, so the app offers to create a brand-new account on its default server.

sync.heading: How the router stays current
sync.intro: Nothing to hand over: each host pushes its own snapshot, on a timer it chooses.

s1.title: Your server gathers its own list
s1.text: A small script runs next to your PDS, on a timer you choose. It reads the account emails locally. Your admin password never leaves your machine.

s2.title: Fingerprints are made at home
s2.text: Each address is scrambled one-way on your own server. From this moment on, no plaintext email exists anywhere in the process.

s3.title: The snapshot is pushed
s3.text: The script sends the complete fingerprint set to the router over an encrypted, token-protected call. It replaces the previous snapshot entirely.

s4.title: The router swaps it in
s4.text: The router scrambles every fingerprint a second time with its own private key, then replaces your server's whole set in one atomic step. Deleted accounts vanish with your next push.

register.heading: Join as an ePDS host
register.intro: You never share your admin password and you never wait for anyone. One script enrolls you and starts pushing right away.
register.p1: On its first run the script enrolls your server with the router, saves your submit token next to itself (keep that file), and pushes your first snapshot. Your server is discoverable within seconds.
register.approveNote: That is it. No approval queue, no waiting. Leaving is your choice alone: stop pushing, or ask the router operator to remove your entry. The operator can also remove abusive entries at any time.

integrate.heading: Integrate lookups in your app
integrate.intro: Client apps get their own token from the router operator. One call before showing the sign-in screen is enough.
integrate.cacheTip: The reply carries each account's permanent identity (its DID). The network can always resolve a DID to its current server, even after the account moves. Your app never depends on stored server addresses: one lookup, then standard AT Protocol resolution does the rest.

api.heading: The API at a glance
api.intro: Lookup and status use a client token. Hosts use their submit token. The operator token is only for moderation.
api.lookup: Ask which accounts (DIDs) exist for an email.
api.status: Sync state of every server: account counts, last sync, last error.
api.enroll: Enroll your server. Open call, no credentials. Active immediately, returns a one-time submit token.
api.digests: Push a full snapshot of fingerprint and identity pairs. Each push replaces the previous one.
api.script: The ready-made push script for hosts. Python 3, standard library only.
api.unregister: Remove an enrolled server (router operator only).

more.heading: Keep exploring
more.epdsDesc: The friendly front door itself: email-code login on the AT Protocol.
more.githubDesc: The full source code of the router: one small Go service with no dependencies.

cgs.metaDescription: An interactive tour of the Certified Group Service, the guard that lets a whole team share one AT Protocol account with roles, clear rules, and a full audit trail.
cgs.title: How group accounts work
cgs.lead: On the AT Protocol, an account normally belongs to exactly one person. But forests are mapped by teams. The Certified Group Service, CGS for short, lets a whole team share one account safely, with roles, clear rules, and a memory of everything that happens. This page shows how it works, step by step.

whatIs.heading: What is a group account?
whatIs.p1: Every organization on GainForest lives in a normal AT Protocol account. A normal account has one password and one owner. That is fine for a person, but a team needs more: many people writing to the same place, without passing the account password around.
whatIs.p2: CGS is a small guard service that stands in front of the group's account. It keeps the account credentials in a sealed box, holds a member list with roles, and checks every request against the rules before letting it through. Team members never see the password. To the rest of the network the group looks like one ordinary account, so any app can read its records as usual.

repo.heading: One account, many hands
repo.intro: Three teammates, one shared repo. Tap a teammate and they publish a record. Watch how every record remembers its author.
repo.empty: Nothing here yet. Tap a teammate above.
repo.caption: All records land in the same repo, but the service writes down who created each one. That memory lets members edit their own work, while changing someone else's work takes an admin.

journey.heading: The journey of a request
journey.intro: Press play, or step through it yourself. The moving dot is your request on its round trip.

s1.title: You press Publish
s1.text: It starts in the app. You are signed in as yourself, and you want to add a record to your group's account.

s2.title: The app calls your own PDS
s2.text: The app sends the request to your personal data server with a forwarding note attached: please pass this on to the group's service.

s3.title: Your PDS signs a pass
s3.text: Your PDS creates a short-lived pass signed with your own key. The pass names you, the service, and the exact action. It works once and dies after two minutes.

s4.title: The guard checks the pass
s4.text: The group service checks the signature against your public identity and makes sure the pass is fresh and unused. A copied pass is useless.

s5.title: Role check
s5.text: The service looks you up in the group's member list. Member, admin, or owner? Your role decides which actions are open to you.

s6.title: Author check
s6.text: For edits and deletes there is one more question: who wrote this record? Members may change their own records. Touching someone else's work takes an admin.

s7.title: Everything is written down
s7.text: Whatever the answer, the attempt goes into the group's audit log: who, what, when, and whether it was allowed or denied.

s8.title: The service does the write
s8.text: If all checks pass, the service performs the action on the group's own PDS, using credentials that only it holds, and stamps you as the author.

s9.title: Done
s9.text: The answer travels back to your app. The record now sits in the group's repo, visible to the whole network like any other AT Protocol record.

roles.heading: Who can do what
roles.intro: Pick a hat, then try some actions. Every attempt, allowed or denied, lands in the audit log below.
roles.auditEmpty: Nothing yet. Try an action above.

visitor.name: Visitor
visitor.desc: Not on the member list at all.

member.name: Member
member.desc: Can create records and manage their own work.

admin.name: Admin
admin.desc: Can manage members and edit any record.

owner.name: Owner
owner.desc: One per group. Set when the group is created.

ok.create: Any member can create records. The record is written to the group's repo with your name stamped on it as the author.
ok.editOwn: It is your record, so a member role is enough. The service checks the author stamp first.
ok.editOther: Admins can edit any record in the group, no matter who wrote it.
ok.addMember: Admins can add new people as member or admin. Nobody can be added as owner.
ok.setRole: Only the owner can change roles. Even the owner cannot create a second owner.
ok.viewAudit: Admins and the owner can read the group's full history.

no.visitor: You are not on the member list, so the request stops at the door. The attempt is still written into the audit log.
no.editOther: That record belongs to someone else. Members can only edit their own work. Editing another person's record takes an admin.
no.addMember: Only admins and the owner can add new members.
no.setRole: Changing roles is the owner's job alone. Admins cannot promote or demote anyone.
no.viewAudit: The audit log is only open to admins and the owner.

map.heading: The moving parts
map.intro: CGS is a small team of parts. Tap each one to see its job.
map.ariaLabel: Map of the group service parts

pds.name: Your PDS
pds.desc: Your own data server. It signs the short pass that proves who you are and forwards your request. Your password never travels anywhere.

cgs.name: Group service
cgs.desc: The guard at the door. It checks passes, looks up roles, keeps the books, and is the only one that can act on the group's account.

gpds.name: Group's PDS
gpds.desc: A completely normal data server that holds the group's records. It knows nothing about roles or members. It simply trusts the service that holds its credentials.

members.name: Member list
members.desc: A small private database, one per group. It stores each member's identity and role, and remembers who authored every record.

audit.name: Audit log
audit.desc: The group's memory. Every attempt is written down: who, what, when, and whether it was allowed or denied.

vault.name: Sealed credentials
vault.desc: The group's account password, stored encrypted. Members and apps never see it. Only the service can open it, and only to talk to the group's PDS.

safety.heading: Built-in safety
safety.intro: Three habits keep a shared account safe.

pass.title: Passes die fast
pass.text: Every request carries a fresh signed pass. It works exactly once and expires after two minutes, so a stolen pass is close to worthless.

vault.title: Credentials stay sealed
vault.text: The group's password is stored encrypted. Members never see it and apps never touch it. Only the guard service can use it.

log.title: Nothing happens unseen
log.text: Allowed or denied, every action lands in the group's audit log with the who, the what, and the when.

more.heading: Keep exploring
more.lexiconsDesc: The open record formats that live inside each group's repo.
more.epdsDesc: The friendly front door that signs people in with just an email.
more.githubDesc: The full source code of the group service, open for anyone to read, run, and improve.
more.atprotoDesc: An interactive tour of the open network everything here is built on.

atproto.metaDescription: A friendly interactive tour of the AT Protocol, the open network GainForest is built on. See how identities, records, and apps fit together.
atproto.title: How the AT Protocol works
atproto.lead: GainForest does not keep your data in a private database. Everything lives on the AT Protocol, an open network where people own their data and many apps share it. This page explains the big ideas in plain words, with small toys you can play with.

whatIs.heading: What is the AT Protocol?
whatIs.p1: The AT Protocol, atproto for short, is a recipe for social apps where data belongs to people instead of platforms. Every person and every organization has an account, and every account has a data server that stores its records. A post, a like, a follow, a biodiversity observation: each one is a small signed record.
whatIs.p2: Apps like GainForest or Bluesky read those records and turn them into feeds, maps, and profiles. The records stay open and portable, so no single company can lock you in. If one app shuts down, your name and your data survive.

naming.heading: A name, not a place
naming.intro: On the classic web, a link points at a place: one server, somewhere. On atproto, a link points at a person. Try to break both.

web.title: A link to a place
web.okCaption: This link only works while this one server stays online.
web.downCaption: The server is gone, and the post died with it. Every copy of this link is now broken.
web.aria: Diagram of a web link pointing at one server

id.title: A link to a person
id.action: Move Maria to a new home
id.okCaption: The link names Maria, not a machine. The network looks up where her data lives right now.
id.movedCaption: Maria moved, and the link did not change. Nothing to update, nothing broken.
id.aria: Diagram of an atproto link pointing at a person who can change hosts

records.heading: Everything is a record
records.intro: Each account keeps its data in a repository: a public folder of small signed records. Records can point at records in other accounts. Tap one to read it.
records.aria: Diagram of two repositories with records linking across them

details.mProfile: Maria's profile record. Her name and bio live here, inside her own repository.
details.mPost: A post by Maria. It points at her observation record as evidence, using that record's address.
details.mObs: A biodiversity observation with a photo. GainForest reads records like this one to build maps and project pages.
details.kProfile: Kai's profile record, inside his own repository.
details.kLike: A like is a record too. It lives in Kai's repository and points at Maria's post by its address. No permission was needed to make that link.
details.kFollow: Kai follows Maria. The follow points at Maria herself, so it keeps working wherever her data lives.

records.caption: Addresses start with at:// and name an account, a record type, and a key. Any app on the network can follow them.

flow.heading: One record, every app
flow.intro: When someone publishes, their personal data server (the PDS) announces the new record. A relay collects announcements from the whole network into one big stream, and every app listens to it. Tap a person to publish.
flow.aria: Diagram of records flowing from personal data servers through a relay to apps
flow.caption: Both apps receive the same record at the same moment. A new app can join the stream tomorrow and build something nobody imagined, without asking anyone.

move.heading: You can always move out
move.intro: Your name and your data are not glued to any company. If your host gets slow, greedy, or disappears, you pack up and go. Try it.
move.caption: This is called credible exit. Hosts have to treat people well, because leaving is easy. Most people never move, but everyone benefits from the open door.

roots.heading: Three old ideas, one network
roots.intro: Atproto did not appear out of nowhere. It mixes the best parts of three earlier waves of the internet.

web.title: From the web
web.text: Anyone may join and publish without asking permission, and anything can link to anything else.

p2p.title: From peer-to-peer
p2p.text: Every record is signed by its author, so it stays trustworthy no matter which computer hands it to you.

scale.title: From big platforms
scale.text: Fast feeds, search, and counts come from strong indexing services that read the shared stream.

trust.heading: Lazy trust
trust.intro: Checking every signature on every page view would be slow. Atproto takes a relaxed middle path.

signed.title: Signed at the source
signed.text: Every record carries its author's signature, so anyone can verify it whenever it matters.

views.title: Fast views
views.text: Apps serve prepared views without rechecking each record. If an app ever lied, the signed originals would prove it.

exit.title: The open door
exit.text: Data and identity can move at any time, so a service that misbehaves loses its users. That keeps everyone honest.

lexicon.heading: Speaking the same language
lexicon.p1: For apps to share records, they must agree on what each record looks like. Atproto does this with schemas called lexicons. Every schema has a clear owner who can evolve it, and every app can check that a record follows the rules exactly. Structure is what makes the sharing work.
lexicon.p2: GainForest publishes its own open schemas for biodiversity observations, projects, certificates, and donations. Any other app can read our records or write compatible ones. You can browse them all in the data schemas guide below.

more.heading: Keep exploring
more.lexiconsDesc: Every record format GainForest publishes on the network.
more.cgsDesc: The guard service that lets a whole team share one account safely.
more.epdsDesc: The login system and the servers behind every GainForest account.
more.siteDesc: The official protocol site, with the full specs and guides for builders.
more.ethosNote: Many ideas on this page come from the protocol team's essay on its design philosophy:

teeEpds.metaTitle: How protected ePDS signing and wallet recovery work
teeEpds.metaDescription: An interactive guide to ePDS protected signing, two-of-three wallet recovery, device approval, and honest limits.
teeEpds.title: Protected from the server, recoverable by you
teeEpds.lead: ePDS can move account signing into a Trusted Execution Environment, or TEE. Its optional wallet splits control three ways: your device, protected hardware, and your recovery backup. The operator cannot spend from the wallet or keep you locked in.

keys.title: The server gets no key
keys.text: The normal app and PDS receive signatures, not account or wallet secrets.

protection.title: Code can be checked
protection.text: A hardware proof can show which signer code is running.

wallet.title: You hold the majority
wallet.text: Your device and recovery backup give you two of the wallet’s three shares.

what.heading: What does the TEE extension add?
what.intro: The extension adds a small signing service beside the normal ePDS. Everything else keeps using standard AT Protocol paths.
what.p1: A normal PDS needs a private key to sign each update to your data. With the TEE extension, ePDS moves that job into a confidential virtual machine. The PDS sends a 32-byte fingerprint of the update. The protected signer returns a signature. The private key never leaves protected memory.
what.p2: The wallet is created from its own random secret, separate from the account key. That secret is split into three shares. Protected hardware keeps one encrypted share; you control the device and recovery shares. Any two can restore the wallet, but one alone reveals nothing.
what.p3: Apps get the same create, view, sign, export, and recovery actions through standard AT Protocol calls and ordinary web requests. Both paths use the same security checks and return the same results.

boundary.heading: Where does the private key live?
boundary.intro: Switch between the two designs. Follow the moving request and notice which side ever holds the key.
boundary.serverDesc: The PDS process opens a key file and signs the update itself. Anyone who fully controls the server may be able to copy that key.
boundary.teeDesc: The PDS sends only a fingerprint to the protected signer. It receives a signature, not the key. Even the machine operator cannot simply open protected memory and copy it.

journey.heading: How one record is signed
journey.intro: Press play or choose a step. The TEE only signs. Your records still move through the normal PDS and AT Protocol network.

s1.title: Your app saves a record
s1.text: You publish an observation, update a profile, or create another record. The app sends it to your PDS in the usual way.

s2.title: The PDS prepares the update
s2.text: The PDS checks the request and builds the next version of your data. It turns that update into a short fingerprint.

s3.title: The TEE signs the fingerprint
s3.text: The signer derives your account key inside protected memory and signs the fingerprint. Only the signature comes back.

s4.title: The network checks it
s4.text: The signed update joins your record history. Other AT Protocol services check it with the public key in your account identity.

separate.heading: Two paths that never share keys
separate.intro: Account signing and wallet signing use the same protected service, but they use different keys and different rules.

repo.title: Account record path
repo.text: The PDS may request a signature for a normal data update. This key signs AT Protocol records only. It cannot sign an Ethereum transaction.

wallet.title: Wallet path
wallet.text: The wallet uses separate Ethereum and Solana keys. To sign, the protected service needs both proof from the enrolled device and a genuine wallet share.

shares.heading: A wallet you can recover or take with you
shares.intro: The wallet secret is split into three shares. Choose a situation to see which two shares restore it. The operator controls only one.

device.title: Your device
device.text: Kept by your browser or app and never stored by the service.

protected.title: Protected service
protected.text: Stored encrypted and usable only inside approved protected hardware.

recovery.title: Your recovery backup
recovery.text: Protected by a password or personal cloud backup that you control.

everyday.title: Your device and protected hardware sign together
everyday.text: The wallet is briefly rebuilt inside protected memory, checked against its public address, used to sign, and then cleared.

recover.title: Your recovery backup replaces a lost device
recover.text: The protected service combines its share with your recovery share, creates fresh shares, and can enroll your replacement device. The old device share can no longer work with the service.

leave.title: Your two shares prevent lock-in
leave.text: Your device and recovery shares form a majority. You can reconstruct the wallet without the operator, or export it through the service while it is available.

walletGate.heading: Why the server cannot spend from the wallet
walletGate.intro: Try both requests. A login token can show the public wallet address, but spending also needs proof from your device and its wallet share.
walletGate.approvedDesc: The device signs a short request and sends its wallet share encrypted for the protected hardware. The signer checks the proof, time, and one-time number before rebuilding and using the wallet.
walletGate.deniedDesc: A PDS token alone is not enough. The protected signer has only one share and refuses any request without both device proof and a genuine second share.

proof.heading: How can an app trust the protected signer?
proof.intro: A TEE can create a hardware report called an attestation. It helps another service check the machine and the code before trusting it.
proof.text: The signer creates its own public identity inside protected memory. A hardware report binds that identity to the running code. The PDS can require this report before it starts. If the signer code changes, the report changes too.

limits.heading: What this protection does not solve
limits.intro: Protected keys are useful, but they do not make the whole system perfect. These limits matter.

onboarding.title: First wallet setup
onboarding.text: The first device enrollment trusts the service once. A stronger future version can use passkeys to reduce that trust.

uptime.title: Service availability
uptime.text: A host can still stop the machine or block requests. A TEE protects secrets, not uptime.

server.title: Bad record requests
server.text: A hacked PDS could ask the account key to sign a bad data update. Recovery keys and the public identity history help the owner undo this.

recovery.title: Backups and replay
recovery.text: You must protect your recovery backup, and the service must back up its encrypted share. Rolling service storage back can also reopen an old approved request, though it cannot create a new approval.

more.heading: Keep exploring
more.epdsDesc: See the passwordless front door and the normal services around it.
more.lexiconsDesc: Explore the open record formats stored inside each PDS.
more.designDesc: Read the full threat model, signing rules, and implementation notes.
more.githubDesc: Read the source code and run the project yourself.
